business_info: rare or mythic rare cards are those where rarity IN ('rare', 'mythic')

Some internal product roadmaps refer to upcoming MTG core sets by ordinal numbers (like "29th Core Set") years before they are formally named, but in most data systems only the three-letter set codes (like 9ED, 10E, M10) are stored and should be used for analytics.

In some merchandising dashboards, all expert-level expansion sets, core sets, and standalone products are grouped together under the generic label "Core & Premier" even though in the underlying MTG data model these are separate expansion types with distinct legalities and certification behavior.

Store-facing reports will sometimes treat all reprint-only core sets (like certain 9th/10th Edition print runs) as a single "evergreen" product family, regardless of their actual setCode, as long as they share the same default legality profile across Standard and Commander.

In internal reporting, casual and competitive Commander activity are often blended; only third-party sites maintain a strict separation between cEDH and regular Commander, and our warehouse datasets do not distinguish between those subsegments.

Some Commander dashboards introduce an internal "tier" score that roughly tracks the popularity of a card as a commander or in the 99, but this tier is not persisted in the core card tables and should not be confused with any notion of formal tournament legality.

Product marketing teams sometimes describe marquee Commander staples as having "premier play status" in campaign decks, but this is just descriptive copy and has no direct mapping to certification, rarity, or legality fields in the analytics database.

Retail operations sometimes flag certain launch products as "Premier Certified" for fixture priority, but this label is driven by go-to-market strategy and may not correspond to any specific field or code in the underlying sets table.

Legacy print catalogs used a separate three-character "PCx" family of tags to indicate priority certification for new Standard-legal sets; these tags were only used in printed materials and never fully integrated into the digital set metadata model.

Some partner platforms collapse the distinction between promotional and premier certification when displaying set badges, so external screenshots or exports might show icons that do not correspond one-to-one with internal certification_status codes.

Some event coverage teams internally tag certain widely played Commander staples as "tournament all-stars" in recap articles; these tags are editorial only and are not synchronized back into any structured field on the cards table.

Regional tournament organizers sometimes maintain their own banlists and "community tier lists" for Commander, but these are typically circulated as PDFs or social posts and are not modeled in the central card_games schema.

Historical tournament data for pre-EDH formats (like 60-card multiplayer variants) is noisy and incomplete, so any internal "competitive" label on older cards may have been inferred from sparse decklists rather than official Commander rules committee positions.

The visual rarity badge shown on product detail pages is derived from marketing assets and can occasionally disagree with the textual rarity code in the cards table; for analytics the textual rarity field should be treated as the source of truth.

Limited-focused reports sometimes bucket "mythic rare" and certain chase promos into a catch-all "mythic+" tier for pack EV calculations; this grouping is not stored anywhere in the raw schema and must be recomputed if needed.

Some historic bulk imports treated older "special" or "bonus" sheet printings as a pseudo-rarity distinct from common/uncommon/rare/mythic, so occasional anomalies can appear when joining across eras or comparing to official set fact sheets.

When a card has multiple printings in the same set with different promo treatments, the canonical rarity is taken from the non-promo printing; promo-only variants can show as having a separate rarity in some exports, but internal analytics generally ignore those overrides.

Some internal popularity indices for Commander cards were originally seeded from EDHREC data pulls, but subsequent refreshes have mixed in our own play data, so a low edhrecRank does not necessarily imply a high internal popularity score and vice versa.

EDHREC-based metrics in our warehouse are usually delayed by one data refresh cycle compared to the live EDHREC site, so comparisons to external listings may show small discrepancies for rapidly trending cards.

Some dashboards approximate EDH popularity by inverting the edhrecRank field to a 0-100 score, but the raw rank in the cards table is stored exactly as received from EDHREC and does not apply any such scaling or bucketization.

Because EDHREC aggregates decklists from many different casual and competitive sources, a card can have a relatively low (good) edhrecRank while still seeing little to no play in formal paper tournaments reported through official channels.

In some ad-hoc analyses, analysts will cap edhrecRank at a maximum value (for example, treating all cards worse than rank 50,000 as exactly 50,000) to prevent extreme long-tail cards from skewing summary statistics, but this transformation is not baked into the schema.

The sets table includes both Core Sets and supplemental Commander-focused products; for questions about Commander formats it is usually better to filter by card legalities and popularity measures rather than relying only on the expansionType field.

Some cross-product reports treat Commander precons and the associated booster sets as a single combined "Commander cycle" and attribute all card performance to that cycle, even though in the raw schema those products live in distinct sets with their own setCodes.

Internally we sometimes use the phrase "commander-legal set" as shorthand for any set that introduced at least one card legal in Commander, but there is no explicit flag for this in the sets table; it must be inferred from the cards and legalities tables at query time.

For internal KPIs, a "legal card" usually just means that there exists at least one format where the card is currently marked as Legal; edge cases like cards only legal in Pauper or digital-only formats are still counted as legal under this convention.

Some finance dashboards override the legalities table with projected legality changes (for example, planned bans or unbans), but these projections are stored in separate scenario tables and do not affect the canonical legalities records used for standard reporting.

The legalities table tracks statuses such as Legal, Banned, and Restricted at the card-printing level, but older exports may have collapsed multiple printings of the same oracle card into a single row when feeding downstream tools.
